skill.name: happyhorse-1-0
skill.description: Generate text-to-video with HappyHorse 1.0 through the local RunComfy CLI. The skill documents model strengths, input schema, prompt guidance, output handling, and routing advice for related video models.
skill.summary: Create short HappyHorse 1.0 text-to-video clips through RunComfy.
skill.author: doany-ai

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer flagged many Markdown backticks, URLs, sample paths, and quoted prompt examples as high-risk patterns. Review found no evidence of malware or prompt injection, but the skill does intentionally run the RunComfy CLI, sends prompts to a third-party API, downloads generated files, and may use local token storage.

medium_findings.title: External CLI Invocation Required
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs agents to call the local runcomfy CLI to submit jobs and poll results. This is legitimate for a RunComfy integration, but it executes an external command and should only run after the user has reviewed the prompt and output directory.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command invocation is explicit and repeated in examples. The risk is operational rather than malicious because the command target is a documented model endpoint and no hidden script is present.

medium_findings.title: Third-Party Prompt Submission
medium_findings.description: The documented workflow sends the user prompt and bearer token to RunComfy model API endpoints, then downloads generated assets from RunComfy domains. Users should avoid submitting confidential prompts unless their RunComfy account and data policy allow it.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file explicitly documents outbound POST, polling, result fetching, and allowed download hosts. This is expected functionality, but it confirms third-party data transfer.

low_findings.title: Local Token File Documentation
low_findings.description: The skill documents that runcomfy login stores a token under the user home configuration directory with owner-only permissions. This is normal CLI behavior, but it means users must protect the local account and configuration directory.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The home-directory token path is explicitly documented. The same line states file mode 0600, which reduces but does not eliminate local credential exposure risk.

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer False Positives Reviewed
low_findings.description: The Ruby backtick execution, weak cryptography, and path traversal findings are false positives caused by Markdown inline code, fenced command examples, ellipses in HTTP paths, and ordinary prose. No evidence found of Ruby code, weak cryptographic implementation, prompt injection, or path traversal logic in the single reviewed file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed file is Markdown documentation, and the flagged locations are examples or prose. The scanner patterns do not correspond to executable Ruby, cryptographic code, or filesystem traversal implementation.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Command Pattern in Documentation
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill provides shell command examples that an agent may execute, including runcomfy run with user-provided prompt content and an output directory. The documentation states the prompt is passed as JSON rather than shell-expanded.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The shell examples are real operational instructions, but the file also documents a JSON input boundary. This supports a moderate confidence risk rather than confirmed command injection.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network Download Whitelist Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The workflow downloads result URLs whose hosts end with RunComfy domains into the selected output directory. This is necessary for generated video retrieval, but users should keep output directories scoped to project workspaces.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The host allowlist and size cap are described directly. The pattern still involves writing third-party generated files to disk.

content.value_statement: Short video generation needs clear model settings and reliable prompt structure. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through HappyHorse 1.0 video creation with RunComfy.
actual_capabilities: Explains when to choose HappyHorse 1.0 instead of related video models.
Documents supported duration, aspect ratio, resolution, seed, and watermark inputs.
Provides RunComfy CLI invocation patterns for horizontal, vertical, and test clips.
Gives prompt guidance for motion, camera direction, audio, and multi-shot consistency.
Describes RunComfy job submission, polling, result download, and exit code behavior.
limitations: Requires a working RunComfy account and local CLI setup.
The documented endpoint is text-to-video only, not image-to-video.
Clips are limited to the documented duration and aspect ratio options.
Prompts and generated outputs are processed by RunComfy services.

use_cases.title: Prototype Product Shorts
use_cases.description: Create short product video concepts with controlled aspect ratio, duration, and watermark settings.

use_cases.title: Plan Multi-Shot Story Clips
use_cases.description: Structure repeated character and wardrobe anchors for consistent visual identity across short scenes.

use_cases.title: Test Video Model Settings
use_cases.description: Run lower-cost 720p trials before creating final 1080p outputs for review.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Clip
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for one short video.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use HappyHorse 1.0 to create a five second 16:9 video. Prompt: A ceramic mug on a wooden table as morning light moves across the room.

prompt_templates.title: Make a Vertical Social Video
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a vertical 9:16 HappyHorse clip for eight seconds. Include close camera movement, clear subject motion, and natural ambient sound.

prompt_templates.title: Refine a Multi-Shot Scene
prompt_templates.scenario: Keep a character consistent across beats.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a two-shot HappyHorse prompt with the same main character in both shots. Restate clothing, setting, camera angle, and audio cues in each shot.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Efficient and Final Outputs
prompt_templates.prompt: Run a 720p three second HappyHorse test clip first, then prepare a 1080p final version using the same seed and improved motion description.

output_examples.input: A creator asks for a short cinematic coffee shop clip with ambience.
output_examples.output: A concise HappyHorse-ready prompt with close-up action, camera movement, warm cafe sound, duration, aspect ratio, and resolution guidance.

output_examples.input: A marketer needs a vertical product reveal for social media.
output_examples.output: A 9:16 video plan with one clear product motion beat, no conflicting style directions, and settings suitable for a short platform clip.

output_examples.input: A producer wants the same character across two shots.
output_examples.output: A two-shot prompt that repeats the character anchor, wardrobe, setting, lighting, and audio continuity for both scene beats.

best_practices: Use one clear motion beat per clip when iterating.
Keep prompts under the documented character limit and use supported aspect ratios.
Start with a short 720p test before producing a final 1080p clip.
anti_patterns: Do not submit confidential prompts unless RunComfy processing is approved.
Do not combine many unrelated actions in one short clip.
Do not write outputs into broad system or shared directories.

faq.question: What does this skill generate?
faq.answer: It helps generate short text-to-video clips with HappyHorse 1.0 through RunComfy.

faq.question: Does it run locally?
faq.answer: No. It uses the local RunComfy CLI to submit jobs to RunComfy model API services.

faq.question: Does it support image-to-video?
faq.answer: No. This skill documents the text-to-video endpoint only.

faq.question: Can it create synchronized audio?
faq.answer: The skill documents HappyHorse 1.0 audio prompting for in-pass synchronized audio.

faq.question: Is a RunComfy token required?
faq.answer: Yes. Users need RunComfy authentication through login or the RUNCOMFY_TOKEN environment variable.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes, with a medium risk warning for external CLI execution, network requests, and local file outputs.
